comments.title: Concerning Transits
comments.content:
With considerable labor I’ve created by hand a chart of my transits. It takes the form of a circular horoscope based on my own, with the transits represented as rays or spokes radiating from a zodiacal hub. Each transit is carefully located by zodiacal position and labeled as to the natal planet or other celestial feature that it aspects, and what that aspect is.

I’m using nine planets (all but Pluto), Midheaven, the Ascendant and the Head of the Dragon. For each of these celestial features I have located the conjunction, two sextiles and two trines, making sixty potential transits in all. I have also located the Houses, not to perfection but within a fraction of a degree (I’m using Astrodienst as my source, and they don’t give exact locations of the House cusps). If it seems useful, I will probably also add a drawing of the aspects between my natal planets, both favorable and afflicted.

My plan is to use the Rosicrucian Ephemeris that I purchased a few months ago to select the best times to launch communications and projects relating to my artistic career, and for other purposes that are important to me. If I can’t locate my Ephemeris (momentarily misplaced), I’ll use Astrodienst to obtain current planetary movements.

Here follows my question. I’m familiar with the astrological basics; I know which planets are benefic, which malefic, and which ones change according to the company they keep or the situation that they’re in. I’ve also been reminded that with transits an applying planet’s orb is only one degree or in some cases, such as that of the Sun, two degrees. I am aware that a planet should be applying to conjoin, not saying goodbye to the natal planet. I gather that a benefic planet (present day) favorably aspecting a natal benefic is bound to be favorable; and indeed if both are benefic, a conjunction is probably good too.

But beyond these basics my knowledge fails. What about malefics? Does a benefic applying to conjoin a (natal) malefic benefit or hurt me? What about the other way about? What about sextiles and trines between these bodies? Does the principle of combustion apply between a transiting planet and the Sun? Does it apply the other way about? What role if any do the Houses play? Is there some interplay between transits and the aspects that relate my natal planets to one another? And so on.

So it appears I need a source for the rules that govern transits and how to use them. Do you know of one that can readily be found online? If not, is there a printed text I should be seeking?

replies.title: Re: Concerning Transits
replies.content: I don't happen to know what's available online, as I'm old-fashioned and prefer print books. There are dozens of books on transits that will give you the guidance you're looking for -- Robert Hand's Planets in Transit is a good thorough guide. You might also consider Vivian Robson's book Electional Astrology, which will help you figure out what placements and aspects are best suited to any given project.

replies.title: Re: Concerning Transits
replies.content: Thanks! I found Robson’s book online (it’s past copyright) and saved the most relevant-to-me passages. But as usual with electional astrology, to meet the requisite conditions is a fustercluck of complications - I’d have to wait for years just for Jupiter to get into a good position - so I’ll try my luck  with Mr. Hand’s book. I hope he doesn’t tell me, as Robson did, that just focusing on any single condition such as transits is next to useless. I’ve put quite a bit of work into this already. But we’ll see.

replies.title: Re: Concerning Transits
replies.content: In dealing with electional astrology it's crucial, as the Russian proverb has it, not to let the perfect be the enemy of the good. If you can get a good aspect with the planet you want, and that planet has no hostile aspects, that's good enough.

replies.title: Re: Concerning Transits
replies.content: I’m glad you said that!

comments.content:
Can anyone recommend any books, courses or trainings on healing? Also on learning to see or otherwise sense/read disease/illness in the subtle bodies?

Also, is the order of essenes work compatible with golden dawn work?

Also, what is it like to achieve the knowledge and conversation with the HGA?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) There are quite literally tens of thousands of such books, courses, and trainings. What kind of healing work are you interested in doing?

2) Yes, I know Golden Dawnies who practice it.

3) Zen masters have the only really good answer to this kind of question. They look at you in that inimitable Zen master-ish way that tells you that you don't have a clue but they're too polite to say so, and then they say something like "Five pounds of flax" or "If you have a staff, I will give it to you; if you do not have one, I will take it away." Either that, or they'll whack you over the head with a stick.

The reason they do that is that experiences of the kind you're asking about are quite literally indescribable in any human language. The words simply don't exist. Imagine for a moment that you were one of a community of blind people, speaking a language that had no words for "light" or "color" or any other aspect of visual experience. Then all at once somebody healed your eyes and you could see. How would you explain to anyone else in your community what you'd experienced?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
More precisely, from what I've been able to glean, Zen teachers are navigating around a pervasive danger of the student apprehending a conceptual representation and then supposing that this apprehension constitutes "direct experience" of something else that was in fact "directly experienceable" without any conceptual representation, or something like that. Also, the danger continues in the form of, the student then immediately makes a habit of supplanting more direct experiences like that, in a particular pernicious self-congratulatory way. (One introductory metaphor is that trying to benefit via such a state is like trying to scratch your feet even though your shoes are on.) It sounds like this somehow connects to some consistent tripwire built into the instincts that make ego images reliably happen in psychologically normal humans, and to how those instincts relate to conceptual representation.

I surmise from what I read that the stick-whacking mostly has a particular technical usage during sitting meditation, when the master perceives that a student has become engaged in a particular inapposite form of quietism. But I take it that that's not the only usage of stick-whacking.

I sometimes wonder whether an applied mathematician with extensive knowledge of computational cognitive science and (several other things and) game theory might have better luck articulating important parts of the structure of such an experience than most people, even if the explanation would have to be only for a very narrow audience -- e.g. analogously for your example, the narrow audience of acoustic physicists for wave phenomena, and the narrow audience of psychophysicists of scent for color qualia. There was a time when, say, there was a certain social dynamic that could only most readily be explained figuratively through the parable of the emperor's new clothes. But now we have concepts that are slightly more immediately-applicable, and so we can articulate the social dynamic somewhat in terms of "preference falsification", which tries to prevent switching between different "strategic equilibria", by the construction of functional analogues to "common knowledge", perhaps in relation to "side-taking" phenomena involving things like "punishment of non-punishers" -- even if all of this is still imprecise in its way (since we don't have great theoretical frameworks for the structure of typical general equilibrium-switching processes, or for the strategic structure of decisions about whether to give salience to propositions already in mutual knowledge). I suppose it's partly a question of just how representationally remote the situation might turn out to be. Also, how repeatable -- it would have been hard to develop the math-heavy and almost perspective-reversal-requiring quantum picture of where classical physical phenomenology emerges from, if no physicist ever got to witness more than like two dozen experiences across their life of distinctively quantum anomalous experimental outcomes, and also all the outcomes were in randomly different experimental paradigms that only sometimes were even tangentially related to whatever experimental apparatus the physicist might have been trying to work on.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
It's not just a conceptual model that can be problematic that way. Nonconceptual, experiential models are just as easy for the ego to use in order to falsify the experience. Imagine that one way to describe it was to speak of bright light saturating all things, the mind empty of thoughts but full of a profound sense of meaning, etc., etc. -- it is entirely possible to construct such an experience in one's imagination, and then convince oneself that one has experienced the underlying reality. The issue, of course, is that the light and the rest of it are side effects of a transformation of consciousness, and have their meaning solely as an indication that the transformation has occurred.

As for your speculation about intellectuals, the testimony of the mystics is that conceptual frameworks -- all conceptual frameworks -- are a barrier rather than a help, and that the highly educated are in fact less able to process and communicate these experiences than others. It's hard to think of anyone better prepared conceptually to make sense of such things than Thomas Aquinas, but when he finally had a mystical experience late in life, he abandoned work on his theological writings, describing them as "straw," and never wrote again.

replies.content:
Sorry, I didn't quite have my thoughts together and ended up saying this in an upside-down order, but: I had some experience in the context of looking of Zen books, of seeing that parts of a few of the things they were using koans to talk about seemed to have conceptual parallels. I mean, the parallels were still metaphorically on the "looking at a tofu recipe" side of the distinction between looking at a tofu recipe and actually making tofu, but they would have been valid as far as they went, and it would have been hard to reach them with an intellectual toolkit that didn't start from bottom-up Hayek-like perspectives on what was even happening in cognition. (I guess it might have been possible to find those parallels from a sufficiently developed conceptual understanding of Taoist alchemy, which is centered on with phenomena that come to attention shortly after considering bottom-up perspectives.) So I thought maybe the problem could have been that people hadn't been coming in with that toolset.

But Aquinas was coming from an intellectual headspace where many concepts relating to high-level psychological and cosmological phenomena were taken for granted, the way even Descartes took the ontological coherence of the subject of the sentence "cogito ergo sum" for granted. So he wasn't a good counterexample.

I didn't know how to just say that, because my intuition immediately mini-panicked and jumped straight to trying to figure out how to avoid socially looking compelled to accept a conclusion that my other experiences had said there were important reasons to doubt, without immediately turning up a handle for what those important reasons were. So I started by saying some scrambled things loosely related to it in a funny order.

What our host just said, in spades!

I was gifted with such an experience when I was an extremely unprepared boy of 13. I remember well the impact it had on me at the time, and I can talk about that impact and my feelings while having it. However, the experience itself utterly escapes my ability to conceptualize, model or discuss, even approximately. Some experiences seem to be inherently "un-conceptualizable," "un-mentally-processible" and "un-verbalizable" (to coin three words) by human bodies and human minds. And that, I have long thought, is very much for our best. "It is our limitations that keep us sane," one wise woman used to say.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
From some other things you've said about the experience of existing slightly on both sides of the veil (which I'm assuming is some narrow feature of the same kind of connection)... I feel like I have a handle on a dim intuition of what that part is like (though the intuition wouldn't correspond to any distinctive memory from this life). Enough to be able to tentatively query the intuition for what might happen if I tried to articulate something on the other end of a connection like that.

For what it's worth, my first impression from that query was something like, "there is an absolutely *amazing* can of worms you'd have to chase down to get that to work", sub-impressions "(all the concerns and considerations are basically orthogonal to the world of your everyday consciousness)" and "(I mean 'get that to work' without it being more trouble than it was worth)", but I distrust that first impression since it the form it took was a little bit conceptually contaminated with a cosmology I was already imagining. (Though it has a few specific details going along with what you said about "keep us sane". Notably, making the concerns and considerations *not* orthogonal, by articulating them, is potentially dangerous due to the potential for creating entrainment and signalling-entanglements. Then again, in some sense, those kinds of entrainment or signalling-implication-entanglement phenomena, caused in more general ways, might be much of why magic or supernatural experiences are even detectably causally accessible to mortal motivations in the first place.) The impression after that was something amused like "you're welcome to try". (Which is sort of in tension with the first impression, unless it's implying that I just won't manage to create any such entrainments anyway so it's not a problem.)

Despite however impressive Aquinas was for the time, I don't really think he had the conceptual toolset that would have made what happened with him an especially meaningful test. The noteworthy stuff starts with, I don't know, Descartes's "well at least the colors a wholly invented nonsensical painting is made from are swatches of real colors from a real world" and Leibniz's mill, and only takes off with Hayek, I haven't given it much thought.

I only once had the chance to talk at any length with a vaguely-semi-realized Zen practitioner (not certified to teach, not that that stopped him) about some conceptual articulations I'd made about certain near-side features of some transformations that are normally only taught using koans. (I mean, I tried to be pretty clear on not having correspondingly realized those transformations myself, at least other than maybe in some back corner of my mind that seemed like it couldn't have come from this life and was mostly sitting things out.) He seemed more positive about my intellectual project than I'd expected. But in retrospect, he wasn't really the sort of person I'd trust to make a wise judgement about whether that was the best thing to say to me. (Or to tell me his true opinion, for that matter. Though at least he would have been lying with intentions he would have thought at the time were good.)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Have you considered that the parable of the emperor's new clothes, or any other inspired myth, might easily contain more subtle insight into the workings of the vast universe (as well as the minute universe within each of us) than any conceptual attempt to articulate a certain social dynamic could ever hope to? Parables have indeed always "explained figuratively", but have you considered how close your usage of "figuratively" comes to "imaginatively"? And would you really want anyone pretending to decode our incomprehensible universe without thoroughly engaging their imagination?

Surely there are many protected and cloistered subsets of navel-gazing scientists who have convinced themselves that they are the best at explaining the complexities of their own tiny area of specialized focus within the larger universe. Unless and until they begin acknowledging all the unmeasurable energies and influences that interpenetrate every one of the phenomena they study, I will continue looking to myth for deeper and more subtle insight into the multi-layered cosmoses we dwell in.

One deep insight that the parable of the emperor's new clothes affords us, which few scientists would be likely to notice let alone willingly articulate, is just how many of our esteemed and vaunted scientists appear to be marching about thoroughly naked.

— Christophe

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I get the impression that you're trying to defend one side in some kind of tension that I don't see and don't understand. I'm loosely aware that mathematics education in the United States tends to leave its victims alienated from their own experience of applying abstractions.

Any single myth relating to a social problem will, if you put effort into helping with your social problem by interpreting it to extract subtle insights, produce a particular schedule of amounts of improvedness of the social problem for given amounts of effort-put-in. The same is true for any single conceptual toolset. And for any particular collection of myths, wherein now part of your effort has to include moving on from one myth to another depending on your gradually-accumulating information about the amount of inspiredness and social-problem-helpfulness-relevance that each myth has. And for no myths or conceptual tools at all. The emotion-endorsement-expressions you are weaving into your sentences seem to constitute some kind of implied claim roughly to the effect that that myths, or some myths, scale infinitely in amounts of social-problem-helpedness produced relative to amount of effort put in, whereas conceptual toolsets do not.

I admit that if I was building a loadout for a long-haul problem, I would have to include both, to get the best returns curve, given convexity in the contributions made by different kinds of subtle insight... But it feels like me saying that is missing the point. Because it's not gesturing towards the important thing here, which is ...

I don't know. It's like you're doing that what-Kantianism-somehow-perhaps-inevitably-became thing, where you're saying some currently-somewhat-sanity-blunted-again version of the 200-year echoing deeper essence, for example as seen in the idealism that animated the Weimar era (and then collapsed into the apparent inverted-higher-self-initiation of Nazi Germany), of:

"Well actually, conceptual toolsets are inferior ontological second-class citizens that are permanently stuck in the lame superficial phenomenal-world ghetto of 'things as they merely appear'. They can't actually mean anything about reality, not the real kind of 'incomprehensible universe' reality that's the only kind that actually matters, specifically because they profess to relate to reality by some intelligibly apparent (and, therefore, skeptically critiqueable as only appearing to relate to reality) means. Whereas myths belong to the real, noumenal world of 'things as they actually are', where all the cool ontological first-class citizens get to hang out. Only myths are allowed to actually mean anything about reality, the real reality, specifically because they do not profess to relate to reality by any intelligibly apparent means (meaning, that myths relate to reality as relating actually is, and therefore do so in a skeptically uncritiqueable way)."

No. Insofar as that's what's happening, that's just be offering reverence to the warped monstruous revenant which Enlightenment skepticism became, and getting caught up in bizarre reversed swirls of its conflicts over the authority of ledger, altar, and throne. Insofar as the Kantian fissure even means anything, humans belong to both of the worlds it constructs, and anything that engages either part of the human automatically engages the other as well.

Whatever's going on with higher planes, it probably only has tangential similarities to the Kantian fissure. Maybe we should treat the applicability of the Kantian conceptual toolset to it with the skepticism that the Kantian toolset teaches us to treat the applicabilities of other toolsets with, "transcendental arguments" or no.

(Come to think of it, one read is that the Kantian divide somehow became Plutonian at some point, roughly ca. 1870...? Does that mean it will stop being radioactive soon?)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
... so then I suppose the thing to worry about with conceptual tools would be, in that case, not really their finiteness or intelligibility relative to myths, but instead the strategic importance that higher minds would put on letting anyone get any misleading impressions about the inherent valuableness of (at least the human aspect of) conceptual tools and conceptual tool usage processes in and of themselves, divorced from context.

But, you know, the same higher minds also have to keep people from getting misleading ideas about the inherent valuableness of the human aspect of religions and religion-producing processes in and of themselves, divorced from their context. And even to some extent about myths and myth-producing processes. So it's not a purely one-sided phenomenon here.

replies.title: Re: Protecting trees from vagabonds
replies.content: I can think of two options. The classic Old World natural magic approach is to scatter mugwort leaves around the area, as this repels unwanted visitors. Then there's the classic New World natural magic approach, which is to do a hotfoot spell. You'll need dirt from footprints left behind by the vagrants -- preferably prints from several different feet. Mix the dirt with asafoetida, salt, and the hottest pepper you can find. (Chinese white pepper is very good for this; you can also throw in a little dry powdered horseradish and some dry mustard.) On the day and hour of Saturn, scatter the resulting powder around the area -- disposable gloves are good so the mixture doesn't get on your hands! -- concentrating on the intention that those who disrespect the common and the trees are not welcome there.

comments.title: Opinion on Geomancy Reading
comments.content:
Hi, JMG. Thanks, as always, for running these MMs.

I could definitely use your help as a sounding board for a geomancy reading on a personal matter. I asked the question whether I can earn more than a nominal income from writing fiction.

The four mothers that I received are (in order): Fortuna Major, Laetitia, Carcer, Populus.

The court generated is: Albus (right witness), Rubeus (left witness), Conjunctio (judge). The reconciler is Acquisitio. So, we have a neutral judge produced by a good but weak witness (Albus) and a dangerously passionate witness (Rubeus). On the surface, not so great. But within the context of the question, I can see ‘good’ in all three figures of the court. Still, Conjunctio is not my favourite figure for this question as it is mobile and is considered by Cattan to be mostly a ‘mean’ figure in this combo. And for creative writing, Rubeus is a bit of a ‘wild stallion’.

For the house chart, I have chosen the 2nd house to be the main quesited as my question is focused on earnings as the ‘end’ (though I also have my eye open to the 3rd house [writing], 5th house [creativity] and 9th house [arts]). At first glance, it looks good: Fortuna Major for the querent and Laetitia for the quesited: hard work rewarded and increase in income. Both very favourable figures. Perfection, however, is not great: F. Major does not pass elsewhere in the chart; and Laetitia forms opposition to the querent by passing to the 7th house and no other house (though it can also mean that it brings joy to my spouse). The ‘secondary quesited’ figures tell a mixed story: Carcer in the 3rd house indicates obstacles and passes to the 10th house, which is square the querent (but trine the primary quesited); Conjunctio in the 5th house seems favourable to me and passes to the 11th house of achieving one’s dreams and is sextile to the querent; and Puella in the 9th house is a favourable figure for artistic expression but does not pass elsewhere in the chart (though it is conjunct Carcer in the 10th house). The cardines are also mixed (1H F. Major -> 10H Carcer -> 7H Laetitia -> 4H Populus) with a neutral ending.

How do you read this chart?

Phosphorescent Squamous Eel (name today)

replies.title: Re: Opinion on Geomancy Reading
replies.content:
As I've noted before, the easiest way to fail at geomancy is to overthink the reading. You asked a straightforward yes or no question and cast a house chart. In a house chart, if there's a mode of perfection connecting the figures of the querent and the quesited, that's a yes, and if not, that's a no. Your answer is "no, you cannot earn a substantial income from your writing." None of the houses you've listed perfect with the querent; sorry.

The reason in this case is not hard to read from the witnesses and judge. Albus is a mild and rather passive figure; Rubeus represents deception, delusion, and hidden trouble. You have an unrealistically rosy idea of what writing for a living is like, and you probably lack the passion and self-discipline needed for the decade or two of hard work with very little reward that every new writer can expect.

It's probably worth saying a little about that latter, because I know a lot of people have no clue what they're facing in the writing business. The median income for a full-time professional freelance author in the US today is a little over $22,000 a year. Yes, that means half of them make less than that; it also means that most writers are quietly being supported by their spouses. (I was, for quite a few years, before I beat the odds and broke into the upper sigmas of income distribution.) If you start writing full time now, barring genius or sheer dumb luck, you can count on ten to twenty years of hard work and low income before you get enough of a backlist that your royalties amount to something and you also have a presence in the market. That didn't use to be the case, and I wish it wasn't the case, but it's been the reality of professional writing for my whole adult life, and it's probably wise for prospective authors to have that in mind.

replies.title: Re: Opinion on Geomancy Reading
replies.content:
1) Most of my books would make lousy movies. Twilight's Last Gleaming would make a good political-military thriller flick, and The Hall of Homeless Gods would work on the big screen. I'd also love to see Studio Ghibli do an animated version of The Shoggoth Concerto, though I can't imagine anyone else doing it without making me want to puke. The rest? The problem with my books as cinema is that making them believable in realistic cinema wouldn't work at all. But if that's set aside, and it becomes a matter of enacting things in a non-representational fashion -- semi-abstract masks, interpretive dance, music improvised around a set of established themes -- that could work, and work well.

He built a center for occult studies, with a big library, some meeting rooms, and a bookstore, and used it as a center for teaching. It's still there in Los Angeles, doing pretty much what he wanted it to do.

comments.title: Q
comments.content:
As a part of my kundalini awakening, I had a sudden thought that qi/prana/energy body is simply the densest part of consciousness. My divination suggested this was right.

It made me wonder, are the planes abstract concepts or are there firm borders between them? How it work (from the mental plane down to the body)?

2nd question, there's a guy who was talking about consciousness and rebirth and said that the world is only rendered when we look at it (he prefers comp sci metaphors), and it isn't rendered otherwise. I'm not so sure about this. There must be a way the physical world that still exists (is rendered) when we aren't aware of it, or in our incarnation? But what would that process be?

Would it be a Spinzoian conception of how we are inside the mind and body of god (with body/mind extensions, and us as modes).

replies.title: Re: Q
replies.content:
1) The borders aren't rigid but they're real. It's like the boundary between microwaves and visible light -- there's a lot of overlap in the infrared, but that doesn't mean that you can light your kitchen by turning on the microwave!

2) That's a simple one. You're not the only being in the universe. Right now ever hair on the back of your head is being rendered by dust mites...

replies.title: Re: Q
replies.content:
2) If it's important for your development for it to have details consistent with a coherent past if you go and look (including something like those details being sampled unbiasedly with respect to how convenient they were to work out in retrospect, when you go and look), and whoever it is that's managing the process is prioritizing your development in that respect, then functionally that's the same thing as it existing even when you're not aware of it. Beyond that, I'm not sure it's healthy to insist on distinctions between "existing" and "not existing". Imagine yourself as managing the process: what ontological distinctions would you even want you to be worrying about, and how far would you want to have to go out of your way to humor you if you cared about those distinctions?

Anyway, the universe might be enough of a plenum that actually there are many versions of different parts of you and many versions of different parts of everything else being propagated in varyingly rigid combinations. (This is just the picture that quantum physics gives us anyway, even before considering managedness.) In this case, the important distinction would be more like "left as an adjustable variable relative to the version of you that's processing the question right now" versus "only has one consistent configuration left to match up with, in case of the scenario where the version of you that's processing the question right now decides to investigate it".

There's things I could say about the kind of "raw" universe where your intuition about "still exists" would just be unproblematically correct, but the concept of "rebirth" there would be so abstract as to be not worth retaining compared to "oblivion" (outside of exotic scenarios or disturbing reframings of heritable variation), so presumably that's not what you meant.

comments.title: Being
comments.content:
I’ve been pursuing an extended meditation, along with lots of (I apologize) AI discussion, of the philosophical concept of “being” in a spiritual/esoteric path. As I call myself a Buddhist (mostly in an ethical sense), I am discovering more and more distinctly and profoundly that I am actually following a Śaivite path — especially as it regards the philosophy of being.

Would you be able to answer a vague question, please, and tell me something about the esoteric and magical idea of being? Is it a historical sort of thread throughout the ancient times, the Renaissance, and in the modern era? I assume “emptiness” is not a guiding factor in the West…

Thank you!

replies.title: Re: Being
replies.content: Depends on which esoteric or occult tradition you have in mind. Most Western esotericism has a Platonic background: that is, the universe of our ordinary experience is a dim third-hand reflection of true Being, which is wholly transcendent and ineffable. No, "emptiness" isn't a guiding factor -- quite the contrary, one of the classic terms for the realm of true Being is _Pleroma_, which literally means "fullness." But there are also apophatic traditions in the West, which use emptiness as a heuristic text of mystical experience.

replies.content:

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
If your friend doesn't know what talisman to make and what purpose to embody in it, he isn't ready to make the talisman. Every magical working, and especially every talisman working, needs to have a clear, precise, and fully worked out intention, or it will fail through vagueness.

This is one of the reasons that daily meditation is so important in any form of magical training -- the mental clarity that comes from regular meditation makes it easier to sort such things out, and turn vague longings into exact intentions.

comments.title: Wealth spell
comments.content:
I've been thinking about the karmic repercussions of wealth magic lately, and this spell from a well-known occultist popped on my feed. Is this an example of the kind of spell that draws from some sort of karmic bank, or is it actually ingenious?

"CONTROVERSIAL JUPITER SPELL
There is a spell I have used several times and it always rewards big. I hesitate to write about it because, well, its easy to misunderstand and abuse.
Still, its a pretty baller bit of magic so.... [frack] it.
Identify something you want but is just beyond what you can afford. Some luxury that other people in a higher income bracket can get but you can't. Buy it. Punch a hole in your budget. If you can do it during the day and hour of Jupiter or Mercury, then all the better!
That night go to Jupiter or another wealth deity you have a relationship with. For our purposes we will go with Jupiter.
IOVIS OPTIMUS MAXIMUS!
O Supreme Sovereign
O Gracious Governor
Whose laughter unleashes lightning and life itself!
Who bestows bounty both boundless and blessed!
Hail to you who is merciful in magnanimity!
I, your dutiful servant have paid you homage on many occasions.
Yet my finances fall short!
I live as so many others around me are able to live
Confident in your support!
Yet my coffers are running low!
Will you let your servant live in squalor while other live in abundance?
Will you invocations go unanswered or will you fill this deficit and balance the budget?>
Enhance my empire,
Open opportunity,
Activate my angels,
Protect me from the profane,
Favor me with fortune,
And grant me your grace.
Hail Jove!
Annoint a Candle to Jove and perhaps to the four wisdom goddesses. Repeat the invocation for four nights. Once each night.
On the surface this seems like being a petulant child then complaining to daddy about what you can't afford. In reality it's sympathetic magic.
Not all magical actions happen in a circle or on an altar. The purchase is the sympathetic magic. Twofold in fact:
1. You are creating a vacuum to be filled and inviting a power to fill it.
2. You are adding to your life the kind of thing you want to be able to afford but cannot. Plus you actually get the cool thing you want. Can't say that about iron fillings on a loadstone.
There is another mechanic at work here, that I have talked about in the past: The Set Point.
The set point is the point of normalcy for you. Whatever that is. When the set point is threatened, our magic seems to carry a bit of extra weight. This is why some people report that when they are threatened with an emergency their spells work fast and true, but when they want something beyond their status quo they get lackluster results. In this case we are upsetting the status quo with the purchase but also using the idea that our set point should be higher. Many others can afford this luxury, why not you?
The trick of course is to not REALLY blow the budget. Don't put your overall well being at risk. If you wind up skipping a meal or something thats fine, but don't buy a car and not be able to pay rent. Got it?
I want to say again: this spell does not work better if the purchase is large or incredibly disruptive. Do not put yourself at serious risk.
Also don't make anyone else pay this. If your spouse or kids are depending on that money for something, don't spend it on this spell.
I did this same spell about 12 years ago using White Dzambhala instead of Jupiter and you know what? Worked like a fucking charm. You wouldn't think it would because of all the Buddhist focus on enlightenment and such, but it worked like a fucking charm. Which, I guess it was. Use your Sadhana instead of the invocation, add some of the same words after the root mantra, and you are good to go.
Recently I found that this spell doesn't need to be worked deliberately. Something came up accidentally and wound up costing WAY more money than I thought it would and it stressed the finances more than I like. I followed it up with the spell and you know what? Unexpected boon snapped into place to fill the gap of the unexpected expense. Yay me but could be Yay too. Never let a crisis go it waste."

replies.title: Re: Wealth spell
replies.content:
It can work. I'd be careful about using it too often, but the mage who designed it was sensible enough to include those cautions in the middle, and now and again, you can get away with it. It's crucial that you already have a relationship with the deity in question, and that you don't overuse it, but Jupiter in particular favors the bold and generous.

As for Buddhism, well, the focus on "enlightenment and such" is about as relevant to Buddhist magic as the Christian focus on "salvation and such" is to European grimoire magic (most of which is designed to function in the context of devout Christian faith). There's a whale of a lot of Buddhist sorcery; in the Japanese traditions, which are most of what I know about, there's a whole class of heterodox sutras that teach how to invoke various powers for worldly purposes.

replies.title: Re: Wealth spell
replies.content:
If you really have the personal discipline and common sense to keep your treat to just slightly irresponsible and go as far as to skip meals if you don't get bailed out, I believe the universe would strongly encourage you to build on your personal discipline by working a reasonable fast or other reasonable lifestyle cutback into your life and having your treat when you've saved the money proper, which if the treat is reasonably sized per the spell, can't be more than a few weeks.

If you find yourself balking at the above, I would strongly encourage you to meditate on whether you're actually sufficiently disciplined to keep this spell reasonable either in frequency, magnitude, or both. In the parlance of this spell, it looks very much designed to allow you to convince yourself you're a reasonable person who's just getting what they deserve while in reality you're taking pointless petty risks and shoving your personal perception of your Set Point to places further and further from your actual means.

I've been a bystander to a similar magical display of indulgence. The person in question made a few hundred dollar "reasonable indulgence" purchase on credit trusting in their job to let them pay it off. They then suddenly got a month discipline at their decent job for a seemingly trivial but seriously taken offence, followed by being let go. They then had to move back in with their family and ask for help paying it off. They got kept on a very, very short leash financially until they paid it all off months later and the firing made it very difficult to find a similarly lucrative position.

In less magical contexts, I've noticed people tend to catch on pretty fast when someone is making big purchases and crying to relatives about not making rent. Their finances quickly become the family business. If they don't improve, the person's character and common decency start getting called into account as the family on the short end of the fiscal abuse try to disentangle the knot of warm fuzzy "help family" from cold prickly "freeloading us out of room and board" until eventually the help stops and no one returns your calls. Might be out of touch, but I don't think I'd want to get into the habit of tiptoeing around getting into a similar place with the divine over a week or two's discretionary income when so many other better options exist.

replies.title: Re: Wealth spell
replies.content:
Apologies for the double post, but another thing occurred to me after I hit send - if you really want something just outside of your means and you already have a working relationship with a generous deity, there's no harm in going to the deity with your desire and sitting down and talking it over. They may help you realize you don't really want the treat that bad, or there may be other habitual indulgences you've built up but don't get the spark out of anymore that would be more than enough to cut back for the new shiny treat, or they could find other ways of earning or making the treat.

In other words: there's no sense stealing your cool parent's credit card when you can just sit down and talk with them (They're cool, right? Everyone wants to hang out with them! You're even depriving yourself of hanging out with them with this spell!) Worst they can do is say no, at best they have a gift idea for later if you really want it, and in between if they have some extra chores they need done, they might be willing to make a deal. Alternatively, you'll have to face them when they find the item on the card statement at the end of the month, and that's not a good place to be. Deities are much harder to fool than parents, and they can carry the bill to your next incarnation if they see fit!

comments.title: Islam
comments.content:
Another querent asked about higher-level souls who incarnate as human to become prophets and religious founders, which you said was a rare phenomenon (usually centuries apart). This led me to wonder about the great founders -- Jesus and Buddha seem to be higher-order beings, but how to categorize the Prophet Muhammad?

My impression is that he was an exemplary but otherwise ordinary human soul who was chosen by some higher agency to transmit the Quran to the world. This would be consistent with the history I've read, which states that the words came upon him forcefully, and he simply wrote them down as they burst into his consciousness. There's also the "Quran challenge", in which a few verses offer a proof of the Quran's supernatural qualities: by challenging the reader to "bring unto a sura like it", with the admonition that neither human poets nor djinn could replicate its inimitable style. While I don't read Arabic, I gather that the Quran is very original, quite unlike the well-developed poetic forms of pre-Islamic Arabia, falling somewhere between poetry & prose, and that it's of superlative quality, with an excellent rhythm & flow when read aloud.

So I'm wondering, what is the occult view of these matters? What sort of soul was Muhammad, what sort of text is the Quran and whence did it come from? As always, all relevant info is appreciated.

replies.title: Re: Islam
replies.content: Islam has never interested me much -- I read an English translation of the Quran when I was a teenager, and decided that if I wanted to take up a dogmatic scriptural monotheist faith I had one handy that didn't require me to take up someone else's language and culture. As for Muhammad's nature and status, well, I wasn't there, you know, and I've seen any number of divergent claims in occult literature. So a not very interested shrug is about the best answer I can give you.

replies.title: Re: Islam
replies.content:
On Jesus, I tend to favor the Carpocratian view on him; that he was an ordinary human, but also an advanced initiate of the Isis cult, and a mage who performed various feats and healing acts that may have seemed like seemed "miracles" to ordinary people. Jewish and Pagan Neoplatonist sources give similar accounts. In fragments surviving from one of his works, the philosopher Porphyry relays an oracular message from Hecate, saying that Jesus was a very pious man of high attainment who attained immortality in the afterlife, but that his disciples screwed up bigtime by twisting some of his teachings and then worshipping him as a god (FWIW).

On Islam and Mohammad, I've seen some interesting theories that would probably be off topic here.

replies.title: Re: OBOD and AURUM SOLIS
replies.content: There was an OBOD system of magic in Ross Nichols' time. As far as I know, though, the order after Nichols' time hasn't done anything with it -- I only know about it because I have some of Nichols' unpublished papers.

replies.content:
Fascinating! Are you at liberty to describe anything about this system? If so, was it comparable to Golden Dawn or Aurum Solis magic, or did it include practices similar to what can be found in your Druid Magic Handbook, such as the Circle of Stones and invoking the Stations of the Year?

JML

replies.title: Re: OBOD and AURUM SOLIS
replies.content: It has very close connections to the Golden Dawn. The whole Druid scene in those days was full of secondhand Golden Dawn magic, along the lines of the work I put into my book The Celtic Golden Dawn, and in fact there are Druid-ized versions of the 0=0 Neophyte ritual from that era that use Celtic godforms instead of Egyptian ones. In Nichols' time, the standard OBOD rituals included invocations of the four archangels, for example.

replies.content: It would be entirely possible, I think, to take the Aurum Solis material, rework it in a Celtic direction, and end up with a form of Druid magic that Nichols would appreciate...

comments.title: Offerings to Nature Spirits
comments.content:
Hello JMG & Commentariat,

Recently I made an offering to the forest spirit who lives in my backyard. I have seen her once before. She seems like a nice spirit.

Recently my building neighbors decided to change the backyard. They matted down some of the grasses and strung lights. They also made a path out of bricks.

I decided to make an offering to the spirit to ask for forgiveness for the changes to the yard. I said, in prayer, that I thank her for the yard and its beauty and I ask for forgiveness for the changes that were made by my neighbors. I left three strawberries in the back yard under a pile of pine needles and dry leaves.

A few days later I had a dream where I saw the strawberries under the pine needles. They seemed static in my dream. It felt like I had made the offering and the spirit had simply said “no thank you” to my offering and prayer.

I am curious if anyone has advice on how to make simple offerings to forest spirits. I’d like to build a relationship with the one that is near me.

comments.title: Planetary charity
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I’ve had a rough year, and I had an astrological reading recently where the astrologer told me I’ve had a number of Saturn transits going. And these are unfortunately going to continue for some time.

Two questions:

1. I know some of the suggestions for planetary charity for Saturn, but in your experience, are there some acts of planetary charity for Saturn that seem especially beneficial?

2. Does planetary charity work if you have a planet in retrograde? For instance, I have Jupiter retrograde in my natal chart. I’ve done Jupiter planetary magic in the past and have felt the results have been underwhelming. I’m wondering whether planetary charity for Jupiter would help.

replies.title: Re: Planetary charity
replies.content:
1) Saturn doesn't play favorites and he's absolutely scrupulous about following the rules. Thus any act of Saturnine charity is exactly equal to any other. If you want to get on his good side, pay off any debts you have faster than you otherwise would, avoid any injustice or unfairness, and if you've got something like that on your conscience, make it right no mater how embarrassing that may be.

2) Planetary charity is especially important when a planet is afflicted, by retrogradation or otherwise.

3) If your natal Jupiter is seriously afflicted (more than just retrograde) you won't get good results from planetary magic with Jupiter. Yes, planetary charity would be wiser. Also, look for a planet in your chart that's well dignified and work with that instead.
